heroOverview: Smallpox was a highly contagious and often fatal infection caused by the Variola major or Variola minor virus, which killed an estimated 300–500 million people in the 20th century alone before it was eradicated by the World Health Organization in 1980 — the only human disease ever fully eliminated through vaccination. The virus spread almost exclusively by respiratory droplets and by direct contact with skin lesions, and it produced a characteristic synchronous rash progressing from macules to papules to deep, fluid-filled pustules in all stages simultaneously across the face, palms, and soles. Case fatality rates for Variola major ranged from 15% to 30% in unvaccinated populations, with some hemorrhagic forms killing more than 90% of those affected. Today no naturally occurring smallpox exists anywhere in the world; the virus is held only in two WHO-authorised repositories in the United States and Russia, and routine vaccination ceased globally after eradication. Smallpox remains a priority in biodefence planning because residual laboratory stocks and the possibility of illicit reconstruction keep it classified as a Category A bioterrorism agent by the U.S. CDC.

keyStats.prevalence: Eradicated; last natural case occurred in Somalia in October 1977; certified eradicated by WHO in May 1980
keyStats.demographics: Historically affected all unvaccinated people equally; children under 5 and immunocompromised adults bore the highest mortality
keyStats.avgAge: All ages susceptible in unvaccinated populations; the global vaccination campaign targeted all ages
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300–500 million deaths in the 20th century; 10–15 million cases/year were projected before eradication was achieved

definition: Smallpox (ICD-10: B03) is an acute, contagious, and historically lethal viral infection caused by Variola virus, a member of the Orthopoxvirus genus of the family Poxviridae. Two clinical forms of Variola are recognised: Variola major, responsible for most severe disease and mortality, and Variola minor (alastrim), which caused a milder illness with case fatality rates below 1%. Unlike chickenpox, whose lesions appear in successive crops at different stages, smallpox pustules are characteristically deep, umbilicated, and synchronous — all at the same stage throughout the body, and are most dense on the face, forearms, and lower legs. The virus replicates in the upper respiratory tract, undergoes two waves of systemic viremia, and seeds the skin, causing the distinctive exanthem. The oropharynx becomes infectious 24–48 hours before the rash appears, creating a window of asymptomatic transmission. Four clinical variants of Variola major are recognised: ordinary (90% of cases), modified (mild disease, typically in previously vaccinated individuals), flat, and hemorrhagic; the last two are rare but carry mortality above 90%. Variola minor produced a clinically distinct, milder illness with rare deaths. The Orthopoxvirus genus also includes monkeypox, cowpox, and vaccinia, which share cross-reactive antigens that explain the protective effect of vaccination with live vaccinia virus.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for approximately 90% of Variola major cases; characterised by profuse discrete or confluent deep pustules with a centrifugal distribution; case fatality 15–30% in unvaccinated individuals.
A milder form occurring in previously vaccinated individuals; fewer and faster-evolving lesions, shorter illness; rarely fatal.
A severe, rare variant in which pustules remain flat and velvety, embedded in the skin rather than raised; case fatality approximately 95% in the unvaccinated.

The rarest and most severe form, with widespread bleeding into the skin and mucous membranes early in the illness, before the typical rash; mortality exceeds 95%. Disproportionately affected pregnant women.
A distinct, milder form caused by a separate, less virulent strain; case fatality below 1%; produces fewer, superficial lesions and shorter fever.

primarySymptoms: High fever (38.5–40.5°C) during the prodromal phase, abrupt in onset and lasting 2–4 days before the rash appears — a key distinguishing feature from chickenpox, where fever and rash appear together.
Severe headache and backache in the prodrome, reported by patients as among the most debilitating aspects of the illness before skin lesions appear.
Profound fatigue and malaise in the prodromal phase, often causing inability to stand or carry out daily activities.
Synchronous maculopapular rash appearing first on the face (especially forehead) and oral mucosa on days 2–4 of illness, then rapidly spreading centrifugally to the arms, trunk, and legs.
Deep, round, firm pustules filling with cloudy pus by days 7–10, appearing all at the same stage simultaneously — the hallmark that distinguishes smallpox from chickenpox.
Umbilicated (centrally indented) pustules, particularly on the face, which become the characteristic pockmarks if lesions heal with scarring.
Dense distribution of lesions on the face, palms, and soles — centrifugal pattern — in contrast to the centripetal (trunk-dominant) pattern of chickenpox.
Painful ulcers in the mouth, throat, and oropharynx appearing before the skin rash, causing odynophagia and accounting for early infectiousness.
Lymphadenopathy (swollen lymph nodes) in the neck and groin during the pustular phase.
Crusting and desquamation of pustules by days 14–20, leaving depressed, pitted scars (pockmarks) on the face and body.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden onset of high fever, headache, and severe backache in a person with a conceivable exposure risk.
Oral ulcers or enanthem appearing before any skin lesions.
A prodromal illness of 2–4 days of fever and malaise before any rash, unlike chickenpox which usually has rash and fever together.
emergencySigns: Any clinically suspected case of smallpox in the modern era is a public health emergency requiring immediate isolation and notification of public health authorities.
Hemorrhagic smallpox: early widespread purpura, mucosal bleeding, or petechiae before typical skin lesions — a rapidly fatal presentation.
Flat smallpox with deeply embedded, non-raised lesions covering most of the body, indicating severe disease.
Signs of secondary bacterial sepsis: new high fever, hypotension, or rapid deterioration during the pustular phase.

causes.description: The primary cause of severe smallpox; a double-stranded DNA virus transmitted by respiratory droplets from the oropharynx of infected individuals during the prodromal and early rash phase, or by direct contact with skin lesions or contaminated fomites such as bedding and clothing.

A genetically distinct, less virulent Orthopoxvirus strain causing a milder illness; it circulated in parallel with Variola major in parts of the Americas and Africa and was eliminated simultaneously by the WHO eradication campaign.

The dominant route; virus shed from oropharyngeal lesions is released into the air during breathing, coughing, and talking and inhaled by susceptible individuals within approximately 1–2 metres, though airborne spread over longer distances has been documented in enclosed spaces.

causes.cause: Direct contact with lesions or infected material
causes.description: Virus present in pustule fluid and crusts can be transmitted through skin-to-skin contact or through contaminated fomites; dried crust from smallpox lesions can remain infectious for months in appropriate conditions, unlike most respiratory viruses.

Variola virus is strictly a human pathogen with no known animal reservoir or vector; eradication was feasible partly because the virus could not survive in wildlife and all transmission chains could in principle be traced to human cases.

riskFactors.factor: Lack of vaccination or waned immunity
riskFactors.description: The sole major risk factor for disease in any hypothetical future exposure scenario; vaccine-derived immunity from the last global vaccination campaigns (before ~1980) has substantially waned or is absent in everyone born after that period in most countries.

Smallpox was moderately infectious (basic reproduction number R₀ approximately 5–7); household contacts of cases had roughly 30–50% attack rates in unvaccinated populations; healthcare workers without PPE were also at high risk.

HIV infection, organ transplantation, haematological malignancy, and other causes of immune deficiency historically increased both susceptibility and case fatality; modified smallpox (the milder vaccinated form) was more likely to be severe in immunosuppressed patients.
Pregnant women faced disproportionately high mortality from smallpox, particularly the hemorrhagic form; fetal infection frequently caused spontaneous abortion or stillbirth.
Young children, particularly infants, experienced higher case fatality rates from ordinary Variola major; maternally transferred antibodies provided only partial and temporary protection.

Since natural eradication, the only realistic modern risk is deliberate release; CDC classifies Variola as a Category A bioterrorism agent, and emergency preparedness plans maintain strategic stockpiles of smallpox vaccine in multiple countries.

affectedDemographics: All unvaccinated people in any age group — universal susceptibility in the pre-eradication era
Pregnant women — disproportionate risk of hemorrhagic smallpox and fetal loss
People born after ~1975–1980 who have never been vaccinated — the entire contemporary unvaccinated global population
Healthcare workers and laboratory personnel in risk settings today
diagnosisOverview: Because smallpox has been eradicated, any suspected case today should be regarded as a public health emergency. The clinical picture of a 2–4-day prodrome of fever, headache, and backache followed by a centrifugal synchronous rash progressing through the same stage throughout the body — with dense involvement of face, palms, and soles — is highly characteristic. The key clinical differentiation from chickenpox is that chickenpox lesions appear in successive crops at different stages (macules, vesicles, pustules, and crusts simultaneously) and are centripetal (trunk-dominant), whereas smallpox lesions are all at the same stage and centrifugal. Monkeypox is the closest clinical mimic in the modern era and must be distinguished by laboratory testing. Laboratory confirmation is obligatory and must be conducted only at WHO-authorised maximum-containment (BSL-4) facilities. Electron microscopy of vesicle fluid can demonstrate Orthopoxvirus particles within hours. Variola-specific polymerase chain reaction (PCR) assays using throat swabs, vesicle fluid, or scab material are available at designated reference laboratories and provide definitive identification. Cell culture and restriction fragment length polymorphism are additional confirmatory tools. Clinical samples must never be handled outside appropriate containment, and any diagnostic suspicion must be immediately reported to national public health authorities and the WHO.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Initial risk stratification; WHO provides an algorithm to classify a case as low, moderate, or high probability of smallpox based on clinical features and exposure history.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician obtains a detailed history of vaccination status, travel, and possible exposures; the examination focuses on the rash distribution, lesion morphology, and presence of enanthem.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive identification of Variola virus DNA in clinical samples, distinguishing it from monkeypox, cowpox, and vaccinia; performed only at WHO-designated BSL-4 reference laboratories.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Vesicle fluid, throat swab, or scab material is collected under strict isolation protocols by trained healthcare workers in full PPE and shipped under biosafety regulations.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid detection of Orthopoxvirus brick-shaped viral particles, which rules in or out poxvirus infection within hours, though it cannot distinguish between Variola and other orthopoxviruses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Vesicle fluid is aspirated using a fine needle by a clinician in full PPE; the sample is fixed and examined at a reference facility.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid detection of poxvirus antigens in skin lesion scrapings; confirms Orthopoxvirus genus before PCR species confirmation is available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lesion scrapings are collected on a glass slide under containment conditions and stained with labelled antibodies for microscopic analysis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detection of IgM and IgG antibodies in acute and convalescent serum; supports diagnosis retrospectively and provides information on immune response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood samples are drawn in pairs 14–21 days apart; antibody titres are measured at a reference laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Isolation of live Variola virus for definitive characterisation; restricted to a small number of WHO-authorised maximum-containment laboratories worldwide.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Only performed at authorised BSL-4 facilities on carefully controlled samples; not a routine diagnostic step for clinical suspicion.

treatmentOverview: There is no licensed antiviral specifically approved for the treatment of active smallpox infection in civilian populations, though two antiviral agents hold emergency-use or regulatory approvals that could be deployed in a bioterrorism scenario. Tecovirimat (TPOXX, ST-246) is an Orthopoxvirus-specific antiviral that inhibits the VP37 viral membrane protein essential for viral egress; it is licensed by the FDA and the European Medicines Agency for smallpox and has a well-characterised safety profile in humans, though it has never been tested in an active smallpox outbreak. Brincidofovir (CMX001) is a lipid-conjugated prodrug of cidofovir that reaches high intracellular concentrations and inhibits viral DNA polymerase; it has demonstrated activity against orthopoxviruses in animal models. Cidofovir itself has in vitro and animal model activity against Variola but causes significant nephrotoxicity and would be reserved for severe cases where benefits outweigh risks. Vaccinia immune globulin intravenous (VIGIV) can be used as adjunctive therapy in selected cases, particularly for vaccine complications and potentially in early smallpox infection. The most important public health measure in any outbreak would be ring vaccination — rapid vaccination of all contacts and contacts of contacts within 3–4 days of exposure, which was the strategy that achieved global eradication and remains the cornerstone of outbreak response plans. Supportive care — fluids, analgesics, antipyretics, management of secondary bacterial superinfection, nutritional support, and wound care for extensive lesions — is central to management of any case. Strict isolation of the patient in a negative-pressure room with droplet and contact precautions is mandatory.

medicalTreatments.name: Tecovirimat (TPOXX, ST-246)
medicalTreatments.description: An FDA- and EMA-approved antiviral that blocks the Orthopoxvirus VP37 protein, preventing mature virions from exiting infected cells and spreading. It is oral, well-tolerated, and is the primary antiviral stockpiled for smallpox biodefence response. Dosing is weight-based twice-daily for 14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In animal models of lethal Variola-equivalent infection (using closely related orthopoxviruses), survival improved by approximately 100% versus untreated controls; human safety studies show response in approximately 90% of monkeypox patients treated off-label.

medicalTreatments.name: Brincidofovir (CMX001)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral lipid-conjugated prodrug of cidofovir with antiviral activity against poxviruses; inhibits viral DNA polymerase with improved cellular uptake and reduced nephrotoxicity compared with cidofovir. It has an FDA approval for smallpox under the Animal Rule.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Animal model data show significant survival benefit against lethal orthopoxvirus challenge; approximately 60–80% survival compared with near-zero in untreated controls in key pivotal studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Vaccinia immune globulin intravenous (VIGIV)
medicalTreatments.description: Pooled human hyperimmune immunoglobulin from vaccinated donors; provides passive antibody-mediated protection. Used primarily for serious vaccinia complications and potentially as adjunctive therapy in early active smallpox infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severity and mortality of vaccinia complications; animal model data suggest a roughly 50% improvement in survival when given with antiviral agents early in orthopoxvirus infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Ring vaccination with ACAM2000 or MVA-BN (Imvamune/Jynneos)
medicalTreatments.description: Post-exposure vaccination within 3–4 days of exposure can prevent or significantly modify disease; the live vaccinia ACAM2000 vaccine provides robust protection to immunocompetent individuals; the replication-deficient MVA-BN vaccine is preferred for immunocompromised individuals and those with atopic dermatitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Historical evidence shows ring vaccination reduced transmission by over 95% and was sufficient to eradicate smallpox globally; post-exposure vaccination within 4 days reduces disease severity by approximately 90–100% in susceptible contacts.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care and management of secondary bacterial infection
medicalTreatments.description: Includes fluid resuscitation, analgesics (paracetamol/ibuprofen for fever and pain), antipruritics, nutritional support for extensive skin involvement, and antibiotics for documented or suspected secondary bacterial superinfection of pustules (most commonly Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Historical comparisons suggest appropriate supportive care reduces case fatality by approximately 5–10% compared with no medical care; antibiotics for secondary bacterial infection are highly effective (>90% cure of superinfection) when correctly selected.

alternativeTreatments.name: Strict isolation and barrier nursing
alternativeTreatments.description: In any outbreak scenario, negative-pressure isolation rooms, full PPE (gown, gloves, N95 respirator, eye protection), and strict contact and droplet precautions are the primary containment measures that protect healthcare workers and prevent spread.

alternativeTreatments.name: Decontamination of environment and fomites
alternativeTreatments.description: Smallpox virus can persist on surfaces and in dried crust material; thorough decontamination of rooms, bedding, and patient care materials with hospital-grade disinfectants effective against non-enveloped viruses is essential in managing any suspected case.

whySeeSpecialist: Any clinical suspicion of smallpox is a public health emergency requiring immediate notification of national health authorities (in the U.S., contact the CDC Emergency Operations Center at 770-488-7100). The patient should be isolated immediately and must not be evaluated in a standard outpatient or emergency department without prior coordination with infection control. An infectious disease specialist with experience in viral haemorrhagic fevers or high-consequence pathogens should be involved alongside public health teams. Post-exposure ring vaccination and contact tracing require specialist epidemiological expertise.
doctorSelectionGuide: In any suspected smallpox scenario, clinical care is co-ordinated by public health authorities rather than being a standard patient referral. Emergency preparedness teams at national public health agencies (CDC in the U.S., PHE/UKHSA in the U.K., ECDC in Europe) maintain response plans and can direct all aspects of clinical and epidemiological management. Clinicians who believe they have encountered a possible case should call their national public health emergency line before taking any diagnostic samples.
preventionStrategies: Smallpox vaccination with live vaccinia ACAM2000 — provides robust protection to immunocompetent individuals; currently offered to laboratory workers and first responders in national preparedness programmes.
MVA-BN (Jynneos/Imvamune) vaccination — a replication-deficient alternative offering a safer profile for immunocompromised individuals and those with skin conditions; also licensed for mpox.
Ring vaccination of all contacts within 3–4 days of a confirmed case — the strategy that eradicated smallpox and remains the primary outbreak response tool.
Strategic national stockpiles of smallpox vaccines and antiviral drugs (tecovirimat, brincidofovir) maintained by the U.S. Strategic National Stockpile and equivalent bodies.
WHO oversight of the two authorised Variola repositories to prevent unauthorised access to live virus.
Surveillance for unusual clusters of febrile vesicular rash illness, particularly monkeypox, which is the orthopoxvirus most likely to be mistaken for smallpox.
lifestyleModifications: In any active outbreak scenario, strict isolation from all household contacts until all lesions have crusted over (approximately 3 weeks from rash onset) is obligatory.
Healthcare workers and first responders should maintain up-to-date smallpox vaccination status if their role qualifies them for national pre-exposure vaccination programmes.
Report unusual poxvirus-like rashes to public health authorities; do not self-treat or delay seeking care if a synchronous centrifugal pustular rash develops with a prodromal fever.

recommended: High-fluid intake to prevent dehydration from fever, particularly during the prodromal and pustular phases
Soft, easily swallowed foods if oral ulcers (enanthem) cause pain when eating
High-calorie, high-protein diet to support tissue repair during extensive skin involvement
Oral nutritional supplements if oral intake is compromised by oropharyngeal lesions
avoid: Spicy, acidic, or hard foods that worsen oral pain from enanthem lesions
Dehydrating beverages such as alcohol or excess caffeine during febrile illness
Any food that requires chewing against painful oropharyngeal ulcers

exerciseGuidelines: Physical rest is mandatory during the acute febrile and pustular phases due to the severity of systemic illness. Patients require strict bed rest and isolation. Recovery from smallpox — historically — was prolonged, with full skin healing taking 3–4 weeks; graded return to activity would follow clinical recovery and full desquamation of all lesions.
dailyManagement: Strict isolation in a negative-pressure room with droplet and contact precautions; no visitors without full PPE.
Regular wound care for extensive pustular lesions to prevent secondary bacterial superinfection.
Analgesics and antipyretics to manage pain and fever; monitor for secondary bacteraemia.
Daily nursing assessment of lesion evolution, fluid balance, nutritional intake, and signs of complications.
Psychological support for the patient and family; isolation during a severe, stigmatising illness is psychologically distressing.
prognosis: In the pre-eradication era, ordinary Variola major carried a case fatality rate of 15–30% in unvaccinated populations, rising to 50–60% in children under 1 year and up to 95% for the hemorrhagic and flat forms. Survivors of ordinary smallpox faced permanent, often extensive facial pockmarking in approximately 65–80% of cases, as well as a significant risk of corneal scarring and blindness from ocular involvement. With modern supportive care and access to antivirals such as tecovirimat, experts estimate that case fatality in a contemporary outbreak could be substantially reduced below historical rates, though definitive data from human smallpox treatment do not exist. Variola minor carried a case fatality below 1% and rarely caused severe permanent scarring. The global population today is almost entirely unvaccinated and thus has no pre-existing immunity, which informs the severity with which any bioterrorism scenario involving smallpox is assessed.
recoveryTimeline: The natural history of ordinary Variola major was approximately 3–4 weeks in survivors. The prodromal fever lasted 2–4 days, the maculopapular rash evolved to papules over 2–3 days, vesicles formed over days 4–5, pustules reached maximum size by days 10–12, scabs formed by days 14–20, and all scabs had separated — marking the end of infectiousness — by days 21–28. Full recovery of skin integrity with healing of pockmarks took months. Permanent facial scarring persisted lifelong in most survivors. With antiviral treatment, animal model data suggest more rapid lesion progression and clearance, but human clinical data are not available.
complications: Permanent facial pockmarks and scarring — occurred in approximately 65–80% of survivors of ordinary smallpox.
Corneal ulceration and blindness — the leading cause of infectious blindness in many historical populations, occurring in roughly 1–2% of ordinary smallpox cases.
Secondary bacterial skin infections (impetigo, cellulitis, or septicaemia) from Staphylococcus or Streptococcus species superinfecting open pustules.
Smallpox pneumonia — a primary viral pneumonitis or secondary bacterial pneumonia occurring in a minority of cases but carrying high mortality.
Encephalitis — a rare complication (1 in 500 cases of ordinary Variola major) presenting as sudden neurological deterioration during the second week of illness.
Arthritis and osteomyelitis — characteristic deep-bone lesions involving the elbows and wrists that caused ankylosis and deformity in approximately 2% of cases.

supportResources.name: CDC — Smallpox (Bioterrorism and Emergency Preparedness)
supportResources.description: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention resource covering smallpox history, symptoms, vaccine information, and emergency preparedness plans.

supportResources.name: WHO — Smallpox
supportResources.description: WHO's authoritative overview of smallpox eradication, current risks, and the status of authorised Variola repositories.

supportResources.name: NIAID — Smallpox Research and Biodefence
supportResources.description: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases resource on smallpox biology, vaccine research, and antiviral development.

sources.title: Henderson DA et al. Smallpox as a biological weapon: medical and public health management. JAMA, 1999
WHO — The Global Eradication of Smallpox (Final Report of the Global Commission for the Certification of Smallpox Eradication, 1980)
Breman JG, Henderson DA. Poxvirus dilemmas: monkeypox, smallpox, and biologic terrorism. New England Journal of Medicine, 1998
Adalja AA et al. Characteristics of U.S. patients receiving medical countermeasures for smallpox: 2003-2015. Emerging Infectious Diseases, 2016
Grosenbach DW et al. Oral tecovirimat for the treatment of smallpox. New England Journal of Medicine, 2018
CDC — Smallpox: Clinicians — Signs and Symptoms
WHO — Advisory Committee on Variola Virus Research (ACVVR) annual reports
Nalca A, Zumbrun EE. ACAM2000: the new smallpox vaccine for United States strategic national stockpile. Drug Design, Development and Therapy, 2010

confusedWithConditions.name: Monkeypox (mpox)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Monkeypox causes a similar centrifugal synchronous rash but also produces prominent lymphadenopathy (which smallpox does not), tends to be less severe, and is confirmed by PCR distinguishing Monkeypox virus from Variola virus. Monkeypox continues to circulate in nature; smallpox does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chickenpox (varicella)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chickenpox lesions appear in successive crops at different stages simultaneously (macules, vesicles, and crusts coexist), are centripetal (trunk-dominant), superficial, and rarely involve palms and soles, unlike the synchronous, deep, centrifugal pustules of smallpox.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpes simplex produces clusters of vesicles localised to the mouth, genitals, or skin dermatomes; it does not cause a generalised synchronous rash with a prodromal fever, and is not a public health emergency.

relatedConditions.name: Monkeypox (mpox)
relatedConditions.relevance: Monkeypox is caused by a closely related Orthopoxvirus and produces the closest clinical mimic of smallpox currently circulating in nature; smallpox vaccine cross-protects against monkeypox.

relatedConditions.name: Cowpox
relatedConditions.relevance: Cowpox virus is another Orthopoxvirus; vaccination with cowpox was the basis of Jenner's original smallpox vaccine, and understanding cowpox infection informed early immunisation science.

relatedConditions.name: Chickenpox (varicella)
relatedConditions.relevance: Chickenpox is the primary clinical differential for smallpox; the two diseases produce superficially similar vesiculopustular rashes but differ fundamentally in lesion synchrony, distribution, and severity.

faqs.question: Has smallpox been eradicated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Smallpox was officially declared eradicated by the WHO in May 1980, making it the only human disease ever fully eliminated through a global vaccination programme. The last naturally occurring case was diagnosed in Somalia in October 1977.

faqs.question: What causes smallpox?
faqs.answer: Smallpox is caused by Variola virus, a member of the Orthopoxvirus family. It spreads through respiratory droplets and direct contact with skin lesions. Two forms existed: Variola major (case fatality 15–30%) and the milder Variola minor (below 1% mortality).

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of smallpox?
faqs.answer: Smallpox begins with 2–4 days of high fever, severe headache, and backache before a rash appears on the face and spreads to the arms and trunk. Characteristic deep, pus-filled pustules all appear at the same stage simultaneously and are dense on the face, palms, and soles — the key difference from chickenpox.

faqs.question: How is smallpox different from chickenpox?
faqs.answer: Smallpox lesions are all at the same stage everywhere on the body at once (synchronous), deep and pus-filled, and densest on the face and extremities including palms and soles. Chickenpox lesions appear in successive crops at different stages simultaneously, are superficial, and are concentrated on the trunk rather than the face and palms.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for smallpox?
faqs.answer: No curative treatment existed historically; however, tecovirimat (TPOXX) is now an FDA-approved antiviral specifically for smallpox that has shown efficacy in animal models. Brincidofovir is a second approved option. Post-exposure vaccination within 3–4 days of exposure can prevent or significantly reduce disease severity.

faqs.question: Is the smallpox vaccine still given?
faqs.answer: Routine smallpox vaccination ended globally around 1980 following eradication. It is currently given to select laboratory workers with potential exposure to orthopoxviruses and to military and first-responder personnel in national biodefence programmes. The general public is not routinely vaccinated.

faqs.question: Could smallpox come back?
faqs.answer: Natural smallpox cannot return because Variola virus exists only in two WHO-authorised secure laboratories (in the U.S. and Russia). The theoretical risk involves deliberate release as a bioterrorism agent, which is why many governments maintain vaccine stockpiles and response plans. Natural re-emergence is not possible.

faqs.question: What is the difference between smallpox and monkeypox?
faqs.answer: Both are Orthopoxviruses with similar rashes, but monkeypox causes prominent swollen lymph nodes (which smallpox does not), is generally less severe, and continues to circulate naturally in parts of Africa and globally. Smallpox is eradicated and exists only in secure laboratories. PCR testing distinguishes them definitively.

faqs.question: How lethal was smallpox?
faqs.answer: Variola major killed approximately 15–30% of unvaccinated people who caught it. The hemorrhagic and flat forms killed over 90% of those affected. In the 20th century alone, smallpox caused an estimated 300–500 million deaths before eradication in 1980.

faqs.question: What are the long-term effects of surviving smallpox?
faqs.answer: Approximately 65–80% of survivors developed permanent facial pockmarks. Roughly 1–2% of survivors experienced corneal scarring or blindness from eye involvement. Some developed arthritis or bone deformities from deep bone lesions. The psychological impact of severe disfigurement was also significant.

faqs.question: Does the chickenpox vaccine protect against smallpox?
faqs.answer: No. The varicella (chickenpox) vaccine uses varicella-zoster virus, which is a herpesvirus unrelated to Variola. Only smallpox-specific vaccines based on vaccinia virus (such as ACAM2000 or MVA-BN) provide protection against smallpox. Cross-protection from the chickenpox vaccine against smallpox does not exist.

faqs.question: How was smallpox eradicated?
faqs.answer: Smallpox was eradicated through a WHO-coordinated global vaccination campaign from 1967 to 1980 that used ring vaccination — rapidly vaccinating all close contacts of every confirmed case to cut off transmission chains. Because Variola has no animal reservoir, interrupting all human-to-human transmission was sufficient to eliminate the virus completely.

faqs.question: What should I do if I think someone has smallpox?
faqs.answer: Immediately isolate the person from others and call your national public health emergency line. In the U.S., contact the CDC Emergency Operations Center at 770-488-7100. Do not send the person to a standard emergency department without prior notification to infection control authorities. Any suspected case is a public health emergency requiring immediate expert response.

faqs.question: Where does the smallpox virus exist today?
faqs.answer: Variola virus is officially held in only two WHO-authorised maximum-security repositories: the CDC in Atlanta, USA, and VECTOR Institute in Novosibirsk, Russia. No other location is permitted to store live virus under WHO agreements. Debate about whether to destroy the remaining samples continues in the scientific community.

faqs.question: What is tecovirimat and how does it work?
faqs.answer: Tecovirimat (brand name TPOXX) is an antiviral drug approved by the FDA and European Medicines Agency specifically for smallpox. It works by blocking a viral protein (VP37) that orthopoxviruses need to exit infected cells and spread through the body. It is stockpiled for biodefence use and has also been used off-label to treat monkeypox.

faqs.question: Can smallpox spread through the air?
faqs.answer: Yes. The main transmission route is respiratory droplets released when an infected person breathes, talks, or coughs; these travel up to approximately 1–2 metres. In rare enclosed settings, airborne spread over longer distances has been documented. Spread also occurs through direct skin-to-skin contact with lesions and through contaminated fomites such as bedding.

faqs.question: Is vaccination safe for people who are immunocompromised?
faqs.answer: Live vaccinia vaccines such as ACAM2000 carry risks for immunocompromised people including progressive vaccinia. The modified vaccinia Ankara (MVA-BN, Jynneos/Imvamune) is a replication-deficient vaccine safe for immunocompromised individuals and those with eczema or atopic dermatitis, and is the preferred option in these groups.

faqs.question: What is ring vaccination?
faqs.answer: Ring vaccination means rapidly vaccinating all direct contacts of a confirmed case, then all contacts of those contacts, creating a 'ring' of immune individuals around the outbreak that prevents the virus spreading further. This strategy successfully eradicated smallpox and remains the primary containment plan for any future outbreak.

faqs.question: Why do older people have a scar on their upper arm?
faqs.answer: People vaccinated against smallpox before routine vaccination ended (typically before 1972–1980 depending on the country) received a live vaccinia vaccine by scarification — a needle repeatedly scratched the skin through a drop of vaccine. The resulting local infection healed as a small, round, pitted scar that is still visible decades later.

faqs.question: Did smallpox affect all races and ages equally?
faqs.answer: Variola virus infected all unvaccinated people regardless of race. Mortality was higher in children under 5, pregnant women, and immunocompromised individuals. Population groups with no prior exposure to smallpox or related orthopoxviruses (such as many Indigenous peoples of the Americas) suffered catastrophic mortality rates of 50–90% from introduced smallpox epidemics.

metaDescription: Smallpox, caused by Variola virus, killed up to 30% of those infected before eradication in 1980. Learn its symptoms, history, vaccines, and biodefence status.
